nameMeanings.name: Reynald
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin): from the Anglo-Norman French and Middle English personal name Reinald, Reynaud (ancient Germanic Raginald, from ragin ‘counsel, decision’ + wald ‘rule, ruler’), first introduced to England by Scandinavian settlers in the Old Norse form Rögnvaldr (see Ronald ) and greatly reinforced after the Conquest by the Norman forms Reinald and Reynaud, which often appear Latinized as Reginaldus. The surname was later much confused with Raynor and Renard . The surname is occasionally also borne by Jews, in which case it presumably represents an Americanized form of one or more similar (like-sounding) Jewish surnames.

variants.name: Raine
meanings: English: from the medieval female personal name Rayne (from Old French reine ‘queen’, Latin regina).
English: from the Middle English personal name Ra(y)ne, Reyne, a pet form of Reynold and Rayner , or possibly Ranulf (see Randolph ).
French and possibly also English: nickname from Old French raine ‘frog’ (from Latin rana).

variants.name: Ronald
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scottish Gillespie.
Scottish: from an Anglicized form of the Gaelic personal name Raonull, a borrowing from the Old Norse personal name Rögnvaldr (see Reynold ).

variants.name: Renaldo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Carmine, Chiara, Reno.
Italian: from the personal name Renaldo, an equivalent of Reynold .

variants.name: Renaud
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Pierre, Armand, Normand, Gilles, Adelard, Gisele, Cecile, Chantal, Emile, Gaston, Germaine.
French: from a personal name composed of the ancient Germanic elements ragin ‘counsel’ + wald ‘rule’, an equivalent to English Reynold . Compare Arno , Erno , Rayno , Reneau , Reno , and Reynaud .
Altered form of French   Arnaud  .

variants.name: Reynolds
meanings: English: variant of Reynold , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s.
Irish (Leitrim): in Ulster the English name in 1 above was substituted for Mac Raghnaill; see McReynolds .
History: Christopher Reynolds of Gravesend, Kent, England, arrived in America sometime before his marriage in 1644 in Isle of Wight County, VA.

variants.name: McReynolds
meanings: Irish (northern): Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Raghnaill, a patronymic from the personal name Raghnall, derived from a Norse personal name of the same origin as Reynold . The final -s is extraneous.

variants.name: Ranucci
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Sabatino, Lia, Marco, Rossano, Salvatore.
Italian: patronymic or plural form of a shortened form of Ranalduccio, a pet form of the personal name Ranaldo, an equivalent of Reynold .

variants.name: Renshaw
meanings: English: habitational name from Renishaw in Eckington (Derbyshire), from the Middle English personal name Reynold + shau(e) ‘copse, wood’ (Old English sceaga). The surname forms sometimes show confusion either between the Middle English elements -shau(e) ‘wood’ and -hale ‘nook’, or with another placename that has not yet been identified.

variants.name: Raynor
meanings: English: of Norman origin, from the Old French personal name Rainer, Reiner, Renier (ancient Germanic Raginhari, from ragin ‘counsel’ + hari, heri ‘army’). The surname was later much confused with Reynold and Renard .

variants.name: Ranallo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Rocco, Aldo, Angelo, Fiorino, Nunzio, Antonio, Carmine, Ennio, Filippo, Sabatino, Savino.
Italian (central and southern): from the personal name Rinaldo, an equivalent of Reynold .

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

en.lifeSummary: Mrs William Reynald was born about 1452, in Bishop's Castle, Shropshire, England, United Kingdom. She had at least 1 daughter with William Reynald.
